articles.title: Understanding Anxiety: A Guide for Young Rwandans
articles.body: Anxiety is a natural response to stress, but when it becomes overwhelming, it can affect your daily life. In Rwanda, young people face academic pressure, unemployment concerns, and social expectations. Understanding your triggers, practicing deep breathing, and talking to a counselor anonymously can make a huge difference. Remember, mental health is health, and seeking help is a strength.

articles.title: Gusobanukirwa Agahinda Gakabije (Depression)
articles.body: Agahinda gakabije si intege nke. Ni indwara ivurwa igakira. Bimwe mu bimenyetso byayo ni ukubura ibitotsi, kumva nta cyizere cyo kubaho ufite, ndetse no gutakaza intege mu byo wakundaga gukora. Niba wumva ufite ibi bimenyetso, watugisha inama kuri Pulse360 mu buryo bwizewe kandi buhishwe.

articles.title: Reproductive Health: Myths vs. Facts
articles.body: There are many misconceptions about sexual and reproductive health. In this article, we debunk common myths surrounding contraception, fertility, and STIs. Knowledge is your shield. Getting accurate information is key to protecting yourself and planning your future.

articles.title: Uburyo bwo Kwirinda Sida n’Izindi Ndwara Zandurira mu Mibonano Mpuzabitsina
articles.body: Kwirinda ni byiza kuruta kwivuza. Koresha agakingirizo buri gihe, ipimishe buri gihe kugira ngo umenye uko uhagaze, kandi niba ukeka ko wanduye vuba, shaka imiti ya PEP (Post-Exposure Prophylaxis) mu masaha 72. Ubuzima bwawe buri mu maboko yawe.

articles.title: Self-Care Habits for Daily Wellness
articles.body: Wellness is not a destination; it is a dynamic journey. Simple acts like sleeping 8 hours a day, drinking enough water, taking short walks, and limiting social media exposure can boost your mood and immune system. Learn to listen to your body and mind.

stories.content: I used to struggle with severe anxiety before my exams. Finding Pulse360 helped me learn relaxation techniques anonymously, and my grades have actually improved. It is great to know I am not alone.
Kuganira ku buzima bw’imyororokere n’ababyeyi bacu biracyari ikizira mu miryango yacu. Pulse360 yampaye amakuru mazima yo kwirinda indwara n’inda zitateganijwe nta pfunwe.

health_qa.question: What is PEP and when should it be taken?
health_qa.answer: PEP (Post-Exposure Prophylaxis) is a course of emergency anti-HIV medicines taken after possible exposure to HIV. It must be started within 72 hours (3 days) of exposure to be effective. Go to the nearest youth clinic or hospital immediately if you need PEP.

health_qa.question: Ese PEP n’iki kandi ifatwa ryari?
health_qa.answer: PEP ni imiti y’urukurikirane itangwa mu gihe cy’imidagadago nyuma yo kwakeka ko wahuye na virusi itera SIDA. Igomba gufatwa mu gihe kitarenze amasaha 72 (iminsi 3) kugira ngo ikore neza. Gana ivuriro ryenda rikwegereye vuba bishoboka.
